subject: Physics
q: When one light ray is reflected from a plane mirror with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> angle of reflection, the angle of deviation of the ray after reflection is:
question: When one light ray is reflected from a plane mirror with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> angle of reflection, the angle of deviation of the ray after reflection is:
solution:
It is given that i=30°.

The formula for angle of deviation is 
δ=180°-2i

Substituting the values,
δ=180°-60°
=120°

The critical angle for total internal reflection is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">θ</mi><mtext>iC</mtext></msub></math> and Brewster's angle of incidence is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">θ</mi><mtext>iB</mtext></msub></math>, such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mtext>sin</mtext><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">θ</mi><mtext>iC</mtext></msub></mrow><mrow><mtext>sin</mtext><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">θ</mi><mtext>iB</mtext></msub></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">η</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mtext>.</mtext><mn>28</mn></math>. The relative refractive index of the two media is
question: A ray of light is incident from a denser to a rarer medium. solution:
μ1: refractive index of rarer medium

μ2: retractive index of denser medium

sinθC=μ1μ2

tanθB=μ1μ2

sin θ C =tan θ B

sin θ C sin θ B = tan θ B sin θ B = 1 cos θ B =1.28

tan θ B = μ 1 μ 2 = 1− ( 1 1.28 ) 2 ( 1 1.28 )

=0.8

solution:

2 R t = t 2 + r 2

subject: Physics
q: A spherical surface of radius of curvature $R$, separates air from glass (refractive index $=1.5$ ). The centre of curvature is in the glass medium. A point object ' $O$ ' placed in air on the optic axis of the surface, so that its real image is formed at ' $I$ ' inside glass. The line OI intersects the spherical surface at P and $\mathrm{PO}=\mathrm{PI}$. The distance PO equals to
question: A spherical surface of radius of curvature $R$, separates air from glass (refractive index $=1.5$ ). The centre of curvature is in the glass medium. A point object ' $O$ ' placed in air on the optic axis of the surface, so that its real image is formed at ' $I$ ' inside glass. The line OI intersects the spherical surface at P and $\mathrm{PO}=\mathrm{PI}$. The distance PO equals to
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{PO}=\mathrm{u}=-\mathrm{x} \\ & \mathrm{PI}=\mathrm{v}=\mathrm{x} \\ & \mathrm{PO}=\mathrm{PI} \\ & \frac{\mu_2}{\mathrm{v}}-\frac{\mu_1}{\mathrm{u}}=\frac{\mu_2-\mu_1}{\mathrm{R}} \\ & \frac{1.5}{\mathrm{x}}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}=\frac{1}{2 \mathrm{R}} \\ & \frac{5}{2 \mathrm{x}}=\frac{1}{2 \mathrm{R}} \\ & \mathrm{X}=5 \mathrm{R}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements : one is labeled as Assertion A and the other is labeled as Reason R.

Assertion A: For a simple microscope, the angular size of the object equals the angular size of the image.

Reason R: Magnification is achieved as the small object can be kept much closer to the eye than 25 cm and hence it subtends a large angle. In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
question:
Given below are two statements : one is labeled as Assertion A and the other is labeled as Reason R.

Assertion A: For a simple microscope, the angular size of the object equals the angular size of the image.

Reason R: Magnification is achieved as the small object can be kept much closer to the eye than 25 cm and hence it subtends a large angle. In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
options: Both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> are true but <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is NOT the correct explanation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>

Both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> are true and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is the correct explanation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>
solution:
θ'=hu0; θ' is same for both object and image

m=θ'θ=Dμ0

u0<D

Hence m>1

subject: Physics
q: A ray of light passing through an equilateral prism is having velocity $2.12 \times 10^8$ m/s in the prism material, then the minimum angle of deviation is _________ degrees.
question: A ray of light passing through an equilateral prism is having velocity $2.12 \times 10^8$ m/s in the prism material, then the minimum angle of deviation is _________ degrees.
solution:
Velocity of light in vacuum, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s

Velocity of light in the prism, $v = 2.12 \times 10^8$ m/s

Refractive index of the prism material, $\mu = \dfrac{c}{v} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^8}{2.12 \times 10^8} \approx 1.414 \approx \sqrt{2}$

For an equilateral prism, the angle of the prism $A = 60^\circ$.

Using the prism formula:

$\mu = \dfrac{\sin\left(\dfrac{A + \delta_m}{2}\right)}{\sin\left(\dfrac{A}{2}\right)}$

$\sqrt{2} = \dfrac{\sin\left(\dfrac{60^\circ + \delta_m}{2}\right)}{\sin(30^\circ)}$

$\sqrt{2} = \dfrac{\sin\left(30^\circ + \dfrac{\delta_m}{2}\right)}{\dfrac{1}{2}}$

$\sin\left(30^\circ + \dfrac{\delta_m}{2}\right) = \dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$

$30^\circ + \dfrac{\delta_m}{2} = 45^\circ$

$\dfrac{\delta_m}{2} = 15^\circ$

$\delta_m = 30^\circ$

Answer: $30$

subject: Physics
q:
A spherical surface separates two media of refractive indices 1 and 1.5 as shown in figure. Distance of the image of an object ' O ', is :
( C is the center of curvature of the spherical surface and $R$ is the radius of curvature)
question:
A spherical surface separates two media of refractive indices 1 and 1.5 as shown in figure. Distance of the image of an object ' O ', is :
( C is the center of curvature of the spherical surface and $R$ is the radius of curvature)
options: 0.24 m right to the spherical surface
0.4 m left to the spherical surface
0.24 m left to the spherical surface
0.4 m right to the spherical surface
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \frac{\mu_2}{\mathrm{v}}-\frac{\mu_1}{\mathrm{u}}=\frac{\mu_2-\mu_1}{\mathrm{R}} \\ & \frac{1.5}{\mathrm{v}}-\frac{1}{(-0.2)}=\frac{1.5-1}{0.4} \\ & \frac{1.5}{\mathrm{v}}=\frac{0.5}{0.4}-\frac{1}{0.2} \\ & \frac{1.5}{\mathrm{v}}=-\frac{1.5}{0.4} \\ & \mathrm{v}=-0.4 \mathrm{~m}\end{aligned}$

solution:

subject: Physics
q: A glass prism of refractive index $1.5$ is immersed in water (refractive index $\frac{4}{3}$ ) as shown in figure. A light beam incident normally on the face $A B$ is $$ \text { totally reflected to reach the face } B C \text {, if } $$
question: A glass prism of refractive index $1.5$ is immersed in water (refractive index $\frac{4}{3}$ ) as shown in figure. A light beam incident normally on the face $A B$ is $$ \text { totally reflected to reach the face } B C \text {, if } $$
solution:
For total internal reflection on face $A C$ $\theta>$ critical angle $(C)$ and $\sin \theta \geq \sin C$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \sin \theta \geq \frac{1}{w_{\mu_g}} \\
& \sin \theta \geq \frac{\mu_w}{\mu_g} \Rightarrow \sin \theta \geq \frac{\frac{4}{3}}{\frac{3}{2}} \\
& \therefore \quad \sin \theta \geq \frac{8}{9} .
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Physics
q: A thin glass (refractive index 1.5) lens has optical power of $-5 D$ in air. Its optical power in a liquid medium with refractive index $1.6$ will be
question: A thin glass (refractive index 1.5) lens has optical power of $-5 D$ in air. Its optical power in a liquid medium with refractive index $1.6$ will be
solution:
$\frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{m}}}{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{air}}}=\frac{\left(\frac{\mu_{\ell}}{\mu_{\mathrm{a}}}-1\right)}{\left(\frac{\mu_{\ell}}{\mu_{\mathrm{m}}}-1\right)}$
$\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{m}}=5 / 8 \mathrm{D}$

subject: Physics
q: The refractive index of the material of a concave lens is $\mu$. It is immersed in a medium of refractive index $\mu_1$. A parallel beam of light is incident on the lens. The path of the emergent rays when $\mu_1>\mu$ is:
question: The refractive index of the material of a concave lens is $\mu$. It is immersed in a medium of refractive index $\mu_1$. A parallel beam of light is incident on the lens. The path of the emergent rays when $\mu_1>\mu$ is:
solution:
If a lens of refractive index $\mu$ is immersed in a medium of refractive index $\mu_1$, then its focal length in medium is given by
$$
\frac{1}{\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{m}}}=\left({ }_{\mathrm{m}} \mu_l-1\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_1}\right)-\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_2}\right)
$$
If $\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{a}}$ is the focal length of lens in air, then
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\frac{1}{\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{a}}}=\left({ }_{\mathrm{a}} \mu_l-1\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_1}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_2}\right)\right. \\
&\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{m}}}{\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{a}}}=\frac{\left({ }_{\mathrm{a}} \mu_l-1\right)}{\left({ }_{\mathrm{m}} \mu_l-1\right)}
\end{aligned}
$$
If $\mu_1>\mu$, then $\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{m}}$ and $\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{a}}$ have opposite signs and the nature of lens changes i.e. a convex lens diverges the light rays and concave lens converges the light rays. Thus given option (a) is correct.

Given:vA=2×108 m s-1 and   vB=1.5×108 m s-1

For critical angle:sinθc=μAμB=cvA×vBc=1.5×1082×108=34

⇒θc=sin-134

Now for TIR, θ>θC

So, θc>sin-134

subject: Physics
q:
An object is placed at a distance of 12 cm from a convex lens. A convex mirror of focal length 15 cm is placed on another side of the lens at 8 cm as shown in the figure. The image of the object coincides with the object.

When the convex mirror is removed, a real and inverted image is formed at a position. The distance of the image from the object will be ___ cm
question:
An object is placed at a distance of 12 cm from a convex lens. A convex mirror of focal length 15 cm is placed on another side of the lens at 8 cm as shown in the figure. The image of the object coincides with the object.

When the convex mirror is removed, a real and inverted image is formed at a position. The distance of the image from the object will be ___ cm
solution: For the object to coincide with the image, the light must fall perpendicularly to the mirror. This means that the light will have to converge at C of the mirror. Without the mirror also, the light would coverage at C. So the distance is:=12+8+30=50 cm.

question: Two vertical parallel mirrors A and B are separated by 10 cm. A point object O is placed at a distance of 2 cm from mirror A. The distance of the second nearest image behind mirror A from the mirror A is _______ cm.
solution:
As can be seen from the image above, object distance is 8 cm [For image by B]

In a plane mirror the image distance is equal to the object distance. Thus, the required distance is

8+8+2 cm=18 cm

subject: Physics
q: Distance between an object and three times magnified real image is 40 cm. The focal length of the mirror used is $\_\_\_\_$ cm.
question: Distance between an object and three times magnified real image is 40 cm. The focal length of the mirror used is $\_\_\_\_$ cm.
solution:
For a mirror with magnification m = -3 (real image), object and image distances satisfy |u| + |v| = 40 cm.
Since m = v/u = -3, we have v = -3u, giving u + 3u = 40, so u = 10 cm and v = -30 cm.
Using mirror formula: $\frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{u} + \frac{1}{v} = \frac{1}{10} + \frac{1}{-30} = \frac{3-1}{30} = \frac{1}{15}$
Hence f = -15 cm.

q: A convex lens has power P. It is cut into two halves along its principal axis. Further one piece (out of the two halves) is cut into two halves perpendicular to the principal axis (as shown in figures). Choose the incorrect option for the reported pieces.
question: A convex lens has power P. It is cut into two halves along its principal axis. Further one piece (out of the two halves) is cut into two halves perpendicular to the principal axis (as shown in figures). Choose the incorrect option for the reported pieces.
solution:
Using Lens maker's formula, power is given by

P=1f=μ-11R1-1R2

 

When convex lens is cut into two parts, P1=1f=μ-12R=P

 

When half convex lens is cut into two parts,P2=1f=μ-11R=P2

Power of lens,  P3=1f=μ-11R=P2

subject: Physics
q: In parallax method for the determination of focal length of a concave mirror, the object should always be placed:
question: In parallax method for the determination of focal length of a concave mirror, the object should always be placed:
options: between the focus $(F)$ and the centre of curvature $(C)$ of the mirror ONLY
between the pole($P$) and the focus $(F)$ of the concave mirror ONLY
beyond the centre of the curvature $(C)$ of the mirror ONLY
at any point beyond the focus $(F)$ of the mirror
solution:
In the parallax method for determining the focal length of a concave mirror, we use an object pin and its real image formed by the mirror.
The method of 'no parallax' involves aligning the tip of the object pin with the tip of its real image. This is only possible if the image formed is real.
For a concave mirror, a real image is formed only when the object is placed at any position beyond the focus ($F$), i.e., $u > f$.
If the object is placed between the pole ($P$) and the focus ($F$), the image formed is virtual, and it cannot be caught on a screen or used for the standard parallax alignment with a physical pin in front of the mirror.
Therefore, the object should be placed at any point beyond the focus ($F$) of the mirror to ensure a real image is formed.

question:

The light ray suffers total internal reflection at the adjacent surface as shown. The maximum value of angle θ should be equal to:
solution:
At maximum angle θ ray at point B goes in gazing emergence, at all less values of θ, TIR occurs. At point B

43×sinθ''=1×sin90°

θ''=sin-134

θ'=π2-θ''

At point A

1×sinθ=43×sinθ'

sinθ=43×sinπ2-θ''

sinθ=43coscos-174

sinθ=43×74

θ=sin-173

question: In a long glass tube, mixture of two liquids A and B with refractive indices 1.3 and 1.4 respectively, forms a convex refractive meniscus towards $A$. If an object placed at 13 cm from the vertex of the meniscus in A forms an image with a magnification of ' $-2^{\prime}$ then the radius of curvature of meniscus is :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{n}_2}{\mathrm{v}}-\frac{\mathrm{n}_1}{\mathrm{u}} \frac{\mathrm{n}_2-\mathrm{n}_1}{\mathrm{R}} \\ & \frac{1.4}{\mathrm{v}}-\frac{1.3}{-13}=\frac{0.1}{\mathrm{R}} \\ & \frac{1.4}{\mathrm{v}}=\frac{1-\mathrm{R}}{10 \mathrm{R}} \\ & \frac{1.4}{\mathrm{v}}=\frac{1-\mathrm{R}}{10 \mathrm{R}} \\ & \mathrm{m}=\frac{\mathrm{v} / \mathrm{n}_2}{\mathrm{u} / \mathrm{n}_1} \\ & -2 \times \frac{(-13)}{1.3}=\frac{10 \mathrm{R}}{1-\mathrm{R}} \\ & \mathrm{R}=\frac{2}{3} \mathrm{~cm}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A convex lens, of focal length 30 cm, a concave lens of focal length 120 cm, and a plane mirror are arranged as shown. For an object kept at a distance of 60 cm from the convex lens, the final image, formed by the combination, is a real image, at a distance of:
question: A convex lens, of focal length 30 cm, a concave lens of focal length 120 cm, and a plane mirror are arranged as shown. For an object kept at a distance of 60 cm from the convex lens, the final image, formed by the combination, is a real image, at a distance of:
solution:
From lens formula for convex lens, we have
1f=1v-1u
⇒130=1v+160
⇒160=1v

Applying lens formula to concave lens, we have 
1f=1v-1u
⇒1-120=1v-140, ∵u=+40 cm
⇒v=60 cm

Virtual object 10 cm behind plane mirror.
Hence real image 10 cm in front of mirror.
Hence 60 cm from convex lens.

subject: Physics
q: Cross-section view of a prism is the equilateral triangle ABC shown in the figure. The minimum deviation is observed using this prism when the angle of incidence is equal to the prism angle. The time taken by light to travel from P (midpoint of BC) to A is ___________ ×10-10 s. (Given, speed of light in vacuum =3×108 m s-1 and cos30°=32)
question: Cross-section view of a prism is the equilateral triangle ABC shown in the figure. The minimum deviation is observed using this prism when the angle of incidence is equal to the prism angle. The time taken by light to travel from P (midpoint of BC) to A is ___________ ×10-10 s. (Given, speed of light in vacuum =3×108 m s-1 and cos30°=32)
solution:
δmin=2i-A
A=2i-A
A=i
μ=sinδmin+A2sinA2=sin(A)sinA2=sin60°sin30°
μ=3
v=cμ=3×1083 m s-1
t=APv=53×10-23×1083=5×10-10s
So, x=5

q: Car <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> overtakes another car <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> at a relative speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. How fast will the image of car <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> appear to move in the mirror of focal length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> fitted in car <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>, when the car <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>9</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> away from the car <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>?
question: Car <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> overtakes another car <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> at a relative speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. How fast will the image of car <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> appear to move in the mirror of focal length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> fitted in car <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>, when the car <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>9</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> away from the car <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>?
solution:
Object distance, u=−1.9 m=190 cm

Focal length, f=10 cm

Magnification, m=ff−u=1010−(−190)

Therefore, m=10200=120

Image velocity, VI=−m2V0=−1202×40

Hence, VI=−0.1 m s-1

Magnitude of velocity =0.1 m s-1

subject: Physics
q: When a beam of white light is allowed to pass through convex lens parallel to principal axis, the different colours of light converge at different point on the principle axis after refraction. This is called :
question: When a beam of white light is allowed to pass through convex lens parallel to principal axis, the different colours of light converge at different point on the principle axis after refraction. This is called :
solution:
Due to difference in refractive index of a material for different wavelengths, the focal length will be different for different colours. This will cause a separation of parallel beam of white light. This phenomenon is known as chromatic aberration.

Polarisation is a property applying to transverse waves that specify the geometrical orientation of the oscillations.

When energy waves (such as light, sound, and various electromagnetic waves) are caused to depart from a straight path due to imperfections in the medium it is called Scattering.

Spherical aberration is present when the outer parts of a lens do not bring light rays into the same focus as the central part.
